content.text: Multi-Head Attention (MHA) is the mechanism that redefined sequence modelling in 2017. Where recurrent networks processed tokens one at a time and convolutional networks had limited receptive fields, attention gives every token direct access to every other token in a single differentiable operation. The multi-head variant runs this lookup in parallel across multiple learned subspaces, allowing the model to simultaneously track syntax, semantics, coreference, and positional proximity. For agents built on transformer-based models, MHA is the foundation of context understanding, instruction following, and multi-step reasoning.
The core idea: soft differentiable lookup

Multi-Head Attention is a parameterised, differentiable lookup: given a set of query vectors, it returns a weighted sum of value vectors, where the weights reflect how compatible each query is with each key. The 'multi-head' aspect runs this lookup
, each time in a different learned linear subspace of the input.

The three roles—queries, keys, and values—map to a database analogy: queries are the questions being asked, keys index the available information, and values are the information content. Unlike a hard lookup, attention is soft: every key gets some weight, and the model learns which queries match which keys entirely from data, via gradient descent.
every token attends to all other tokens in the input—no masking. Used in BERT-style models for bidirectional context understanding.
each position can only attend to positions at or before it. A triangular mask of
ensures no information flows from future tokens. Used in GPT-style autoregressive generation.
decoder queries attend to encoder key-value pairs. The decoder 'reads' the encoder's representations through attention, with no direct token-to-token connections.
Before attention, the primary bottleneck in sequence models was the

: an RNN compresses an entire source sequence into a fixed-size hidden state, losing long-range dependencies for long inputs. Attention eliminates this bottleneck—every token has a direct, learned connection to every other token, regardless of distance in the sequence.

For agentic AI, MHA is the mechanism by which a model simultaneously attends to the system prompt, the user's current message, recent tool call outputs, and earlier reasoning steps. Without MHA, an agent could not reliably ground tool selection on context established many tokens ago. The ability to attend across thousands of tokens in a single layer is what enables modern agents to maintain coherent multi-step plans.

MHA also enables interpretability research: attention weight matrices are directly inspectable, making it possible to study what information each layer uses. Research on attention head specialisation (e.g., Clark et al. 2019 on BERT) shows that individual heads systematically learn to track syntactic dependencies, coreference, and positional relationships—entirely without explicit supervision.
every position attends to every other position in O(1) computation steps, regardless of sequence length.
all positions are computed simultaneously, with no sequential dependency as in RNNs. This enables efficient GPU utilisation during training.
h heads capture different linguistic and structural phenomena simultaneously in the same layer, without hand-engineering.
projection matrices, attention logits, and value aggregation are all differentiable, enabling joint optimisation by backpropagation.
attention reduces to batched matrix multiplications (GEMM), which GPUs and TPUs are highly optimised for through cuBLAS and XLA.
attention weights can be visualised and analysed to understand model decision-making, supporting debugging and safety research.
naive attention is O(n²) in sequence length, both in memory (storing the
attention matrix) and in compute. For n=32,768, a single attention matrix in fp32 requires 4 GB.
attention is permutation-equivariant by design; positional encodings (absolute, relative, or RoPE) must be added separately.
autoregressive decoding caches all past K, V tensors; memory grows linearly with context length and batch size.
standard MHA does not generalise beyond sequence lengths seen during training without positional encoding modifications like RoPE or ALiBi.
The fundamental operation is scaled dot-product attention. Given query matrix
prevents dot products from growing large as
increases, which would push the softmax into regions of near-zero gradient. Without it, large
causes training instability via vanishing softmax gradients.
Multi-Head Attention projects Q, K, V into h separate lower-dimensional subspaces and runs scaled dot-product attention independently in each:
. The head outputs are concatenated and projected:
, the output has the same shape as the input, maintaining the residual stream dimension throughout the network.
The four projection matrices contribute the following per-layer parameters:
this is roughly 1M parameters per MHA layer. In typical transformer configurations, MHA and FFN layers each hold comparable parameter counts.

content.text: for encoder-only tasks: text classification, token labelling, question answering over a provided context.
for autoregressive generation: language modelling, code generation, and agent action sequence prediction.
when conditioning a decoder on a separate context: translation, summarisation, retrieval-augmented generation (retrieved passages become keys and values).
whenever sequence lengths exceed 2,048 tokens; the IO-aware algorithm reduces memory from O(n²) to O(n) while preserving exact computation.
for long-context inference to reduce KV-cache memory with minor quality cost.
Reduce the number of heads for small models where memory is tight; increase heads for large models where each head can specialise on distinct patterns.
The following is a complete from-scratch implementation of Multi-Head Attention in PyTorch, supporting self-attention, cross-attention, and optional masking:

import math
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class ScaledDotProductAttention(nn.Module):
    """Core attention: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) V"""
    def forward(self, q, k, v, mask=None):
        d_k = q.size(-1)
        # (B, h, T_q, d_k) x (B, h, d_k, T_k) -> (B, h, T_q, T_k)
        scores = torch.matmul(q, k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(d_k)
        if mask is not None:
            scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float("-inf"))
        weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)   # attention distribution
        return torch.matmul(weights, v), weights

class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    """Multi-Head Attention from Vaswani et al. (2017).

    Projects Q, K, V into h subspaces, runs scaled dot-product attention
    in each subspace in parallel, then concatenates and re-projects.
    """
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % num_heads == 0, "d_model must be divisible by num_heads"
        self.d_model = d_model
        self.h = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads          # dimension per head

        self.W_q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_k = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_v = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_o = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)
        self.attn = ScaledDotProductAttention()

    def _split_heads(self, x):
        """(B, T, d_model) -> (B, h, T, d_k)"""
        B, T, _ = x.shape
        return x.view(B, T, self.h, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)

    def forward(self, query, key, value, mask=None):
        """
        query: (B, T_q, d_model)
        key:   (B, T_k, d_model)
        value: (B, T_v, d_model)  -- T_k == T_v always
        mask:  optional (B, 1, T_q, T_k) or (T_q, T_k)
        """
        B = query.size(0)
        q = self._split_heads(self.W_q(query))   # (B, h, T_q, d_k)
        k = self._split_heads(self.W_k(key))     # (B, h, T_k, d_k)
        v = self._split_heads(self.W_v(value))   # (B, h, T_v, d_k)

        out, self.last_weights = self.attn(q, k, v, mask)
        # (B, h, T_q, d_k) -> (B, T_q, h*d_k) = (B, T_q, d_model)
        out = out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, -1, self.d_model)
        return self.W_o(out)

# --- Smoke test ---
if __name__ == "__main__":
    mha = MultiHeadAttention(d_model=512, num_heads=8)
    x = torch.randn(2, 10, 512)       # batch=2, seq=10, d_model=512
    out = mha(x, x, x)               # self-attention
    print("Self-attn output:", out.shape)   # (2, 10, 512)

    # Cross-attention: decoder attends to encoder
    enc = torch.randn(2, 20, 512)    # encoder seq_len=20
    dec = torch.randn(2, 5, 512)     # decoder seq_len=5
    out_cross = mha(dec, enc, enc)
    print("Cross-attn output:", out_cross.shape)  # (2, 5, 512)
which uses optimised fused kernels and handles edge cases correctly:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Using PyTorch built-in MultiheadAttention (recommended for production)
mha = nn.MultiheadAttention(
    embed_dim=512,
    num_heads=8,
    dropout=0.1,
    batch_first=True,    # (batch, seq, embed) -- modern convention
)

# Self-attention
x = torch.randn(2, 10, 512)
out, attn_weights = mha(x, x, x)
print("Output shape      :", out.shape)          # (2, 10, 512)
print("Attn weights shape:", attn_weights.shape) # (2, 10, 10)

# Cross-attention: decoder queries attend to encoder key/value
encoder_out = torch.randn(2, 20, 512)
decoder_q   = torch.randn(2, 5, 512)
out_cross, _ = mha(decoder_q, encoder_out, encoder_out)
print("Cross-attn output :", out_cross.shape)   # (2, 5, 512)

# Causal / autoregressive mask (for decoder self-attention)
T = 10
causal_mask = nn.Transformer.generate_square_subsequent_mask(T)
# causal_mask: (T, T) with -inf in upper triangle
out_causal, _ = mha(x, x, x, attn_mask=causal_mask)
print("Causal output     :", out_causal.shape)  # (2, 10, 512)

# Key padding mask: ignore PAD tokens (True = ignore that position)
key_padding = torch.zeros(2, 10, dtype=torch.bool)
key_padding[0, 7:] = True  # batch item 0: last 3 tokens are padding
out_padded, _ = mha(x, x, x, key_padding_mask=key_padding)
print("Padded output     :", out_padded.shape)  # (2, 10, 512)
Monitoring the entropy of attention distributions reveals head specialisation and detects head collapse during training:

import math
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def compute_attention_entropy(weights: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Compute per-head entropy of attention distributions.
    weights: (batch, heads, T_q, T_k)
    Returns: (batch, heads) mean entropy across query positions.
    """
    eps = 1e-9
    # Entropy H = -sum(p * log(p)) across T_k dimension
    entropy = -(weights * (weights + eps).log()).sum(dim=-1)  # (B, h, T_q)
    return entropy.mean(dim=-1)  # (B, h) -- mean over query positions

# Simulate attention weights for analysis
torch.manual_seed(42)
batch, heads, T = 2, 8, 12
# Random weights in [0,1] summing to 1 over T_k dimension
raw = torch.rand(batch, heads, T, T)
weights = F.softmax(raw, dim=-1)

entropy = compute_attention_entropy(weights)
print("Attention entropy per head (batch item 0):")
for h_idx in range(heads):
    h_entropy = entropy[0, h_idx].item()
    max_entropy = math.log(T)  # uniform distribution
    uniformity = h_entropy / max_entropy
    print(f"  Head {h_idx+1}: entropy={h_entropy:.3f} "
          f"(uniformity={uniformity:.1%})")

# Low uniformity (<30%) = head is highly selective (focuses on a few tokens)
# High uniformity (>80%) = head distributes attention broadly
# Head collapse: multiple heads with identical high uniformity -> redundant heads
# Tip: in training, monitor per-head entropy to detect collapse early.

. Systematic analysis of attention patterns (Clark et al. 2019; Voita et al. 2019) shows clear specialisation: specific heads track direct object relationships, others follow the previous token (important for language model fluency), and some attend to sentence-final punctuation regardless of position.

In Claude-style agentic models, cross-attention between tool call outputs and the ongoing generation is what enables reliable parameter extraction and context-grounded reasoning. When a user asks a multi-step question and the agent has invoked a search tool, the attention mechanism must route the search result tokens' representations to the exact positions in the generated response where they are needed—a task MHA accomplishes through learned W_Q, W_K projections that align question positions with answer-containing positions.
(shape T×T); the padding mask goes to
(shape B×T). Swapping these produces silently wrong outputs, not runtime errors.
forgetting the scale factor causes attention logits to have variance proportional to d_k, pushing softmax into vanishing-gradient regions. This is particularly damaging for large d_k (e.g., d_k=128).

in Pre-LN transformers, projection matrices are typically bias-free since the immediately preceding LayerNorm provides centering. Adding bias doubles initialisation surface and can introduce scale instability.

in encoder-decoder cross-attention, K and V come from the encoder output; Q comes from the decoder. A common error passes the decoder state as key or value, causing the model to attend to itself instead of the encoder.

during autoregressive generation with caching, the new token's query must attend only up to and including the current position. Off-by-one errors produce incoherent generation without raising an exception.
Not handling the case where T_k != T_q:
cross-attention has different sequence lengths for queries and keys. Code that assumes square attention matrices will fail silently or crash depending on how the mismatch manifests.
Initialise projection matrices with Xavier uniform (std
) to keep output variance near 1.0 at initialisation.
in PyTorch's built-in MHA—the default (seq, batch, embed) convention requires explicit transpositions that are easy to forget.
(PyTorch 2.0+), which auto-dispatches to the fastest available kernel on the current hardware.
Monitor attention entropy per head during training; entropy dropping to near-zero uniformly across heads indicates head collapse—add attention dropout or auxiliary diversity loss.
For LoRA fine-tuning, target all four projection matrices (W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O) for maximum expressiveness; targeting only W_Q and W_V works for smaller adapters.
around the MHA module for 1.3–2× inference speedup on A100/H100 via Triton kernel fusion.
The dominant cost of standard attention is

for the QK^T matrix multiply and the value aggregation. For n=8,192 tokens with d_model=1,024, a single MHA layer requires roughly 8 GB of intermediate activation memory in fp32, making naive implementation infeasible for long contexts.

Flash Attention (Dao et al. 2022) solves this by tiling the attention computation to fit in SRAM and recomputing blocks during backpropagation rather than materialising the full n×n matrix. The result is O(n) HBM memory with identical numerical output, enabling 32K+ token contexts on a single A100 GPU. Flash Attention 2 achieves 50–73% of peak A100 FLOPs for sequences of 4K–32K tokens.
For inference, the KV-cache memory grows as

(K and V tensors across n context positions and L layers). Grouped-query attention (GQA) reduces this by a factor of h/g where g is the number of key-value groups, allowing Llama-3 to serve 128K context with manageable memory.
PyTorch built-in, battle-tested for production. Set
for the modern (batch, seq, embed) convention.
lower-level function that dispatches to Flash Attention, memory-efficient attention, or standard math attention based on hardware and sequence length.
Tri Dao's optimised CUDA implementation; use for training on sequences longer than 2K or whenever memory bandwidth is the bottleneck.
Meta's library of efficient attention variants: memory-efficient attention, local attention with sliding windows, rotary position embeddings.
on any AutoModel; visualise with BertViz.
interactive browser tool for visualising multi-head attention patterns by layer and head, supporting all major HuggingFace model families.
MHA is not the only sequence mixing mechanism. The table below compares the most relevant alternatives on key dimensions:
Slight quality loss vs full MHA

Attention matrices are a primary target for prompt injection attacks. Adversarially crafted tokens can exploit the attention mechanism to hijack the model's context window: a sufficiently salient injected token captures near-100% attention weight from subsequent positions, effectively suppressing the original context. This is the mechanistic basis for many indirect prompt injection attacks on agents.

A practical detection signal is the distribution of attention entropy: legitimate inputs produce moderate-entropy attention patterns, while injection attacks often produce anomalously low-entropy patterns (a single position receiving near-100% weight across many heads and layers). Production agent systems should monitor attention statistics and apply structural context isolation—placing tool outputs, user input, and system prompts in explicitly separated contexts—rather than relying solely on attention as the security boundary.

against the same sequence simultaneously. Each head learns to search for a different type of relationship: one finds grammatical subjects for verbs, another tracks pronoun antecedents, a third identifies semantically similar tokens. Because all heads share the same input representations but project them into different subspaces, each head independently constructs its own relevance notion from the same raw data.
The output projection W^O is the

: it learns how to weight and recombine the answers from all h heads into a single updated token representation. This makes MHA a form of parallel ensemble of attention mechanisms, where the model implicitly learns to specialise each ensemble member toward a complementary aspect of the input, all from end-to-end training without explicit head role assignments.
MHA runs h attention functions in parallel in different learned subspaces, capturing multiple types of token relationships simultaneously without hand-engineering.
where each head uses its own projection matrices.
scaling factor is critical: omitting it causes vanishing softmax gradients for large head dimensions.
Standard attention is O(n²) in sequence length; Flash Attention achieves the same result in O(n) HBM memory via tiled SRAM computation.
Three variants exist: bidirectional self-attention (encoder), causal self-attention (decoder), and cross-attention (encoder-decoder).
which auto-dispatches to the fastest available kernel.
Attention entropy monitoring detects head collapse during training and can signal adversarial prompt injection at runtime.
Individual heads learn interpretable specialisations (syntactic, semantic, positional) entirely from end-to-end training.
